label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is about what we can infer from the headline "Safina goes from basket case to top of the world." The options are A, B, C, D. First, I need to understand the context. The article talks about Dinara Safina becoming the world No.1 in tennis. The headline is from the Sydney Morning Herald during the Australian Open. The article mentions that her emotional state is volatile, and the headline is said to sum up her journey. A "basket case" usually refers to someone who is very nervous, emotionally unstable, or in a bad state. "Top of the world" would mean the highest rank or achievement. So the headline is contrasting her previous emotional struggles or instability ("basket case") with her current success ("top of the world"). Looking at the options: A) Safina wanted to top the world when she was a baby in a basket. That's a literal interpretation of "basket case" as a baby in a basket, which doesn't make sense. The phrase is idiomatic, so A is probably incorrect. B) After winning the Australian Open, she cried. The headline doesn't mention crying, just the transformation from a difficult/emotional state to success. So B might not be right. C) Too nervous at first but gained confidence and won. This fits with the idea of starting as a "basket case" (nervous/emotionally unstable) and then becoming successful. The article also mentions her emotional explosiveness and that her emotional state was described as "mad as a snake," which supports the idea of initial nervousness turning into confidence. D) She learned from the basket case. The phrase "basket case" here is about her emotional state, not someone else she
